front: Work: in this novel, the protagonist uses numerology to determine he is destined to kill an invading general he calls the Antichrist
back: War and Peace (Leo Tolstoy) — Pierre Bezukhov

front: Work: the epilogue of this novel argues against the Great Man theory of history, claiming 'the movement of the nations' is caused by collective activity
Work: Isaiah Berlin's 'The Hedgehog and the Fox' argues this author failed to express 'one big thing' in his theory of history
Work: men leading dogs cry 'ulyulyu!' during a family's elaborate wolf-hunt in this novel
Work: Dolokhov gambles away 40,000 rubles after proposing to Sonya in this novel
Work: Pierre joins a Russian artillery crew to observe the Battle of Borodino, which this novel casts as a moral victory
Work: Vronsky breaks his horse Frou-Frou's back during a steeplechase in this novel, revealing his affair
Work: this character regains his faith after a lightning storm threatens his family and a peasant named Fyodor talks to him
Work: this character, an authorial stand-in, proposes marriage by writing first letters of words on a chalk table
Work: Anna's brother continually cheats on his wife Dolly and plays matchmaker for Levin and Kitty
Work: in this novel, a character attends a disappointing orchestral adaptation of King Lear, expressing the author's distaste for the play
Work: the title character of this novella ponders the syllogism 'Caius is a man, men are mortal, therefore Caius is mortal'
back: The Death of Ivan Ilyich (Leo Tolstoy)

front: Work: this novella opens with men playing bridge/cards at a funeral; one character (Schwartz) is keen to play cards
back: The Death of Ivan Ilyich (Leo Tolstoy)

front: Work: in this story, Pahom is promised all the Bashkir land his spade can touch by sunset; he collapses and receives only six feet for his grave
back: "How Much Land Does a Man Need?" (Leo Tolstoy)

front: Work: in this story, Pahom dreams of the chief transforming into a peasant, then the Devil, before dying the next day
back: "How Much Land Does a Man Need?" (Leo Tolstoy)

front: Work: a hermit cuts off his own finger to resist the seductive efforts of Makovkina, who joins a convent in response
Work: this title character is named for crockery he breaks as a child; dies clearing snow off a roof after his father prevents him from marrying cook Ustinya
Work: in this story, a man buys the Lives of the Saints with money earned making boots and refuses to reveal who dug an escape tunnel
back: "God Sees the Truth, But Waits" (Leo Tolstoy)

front: Work: Viktor Shklovsky praised this story told from an old horse's point of view as an example of 'defamiliarization' in 'Art as Device'
Work: in this story, a man walks in circles, yells 'I'm coming!' and lies on his servant to save him from freezing in a snowstorm
Work: a red thistle which lost its beauty when plucked is compared to a Muslim freedom fighter in this story
Work: the insanely jealous Pozdnyshev kills his wife after her affair with violinist Troukhatchevsky, who hides under a piano
Work: this novella's appended epilogue advocates complete chastity despite the author having thirteen children
Work: this treatise on Christian anarchism was banned in Russia and concludes that no Christian nation can go to war; its title quotes Luke 17:21
back: The Kingdom of God Is Within You (Leo Tolstoy)
work: The Kingdom of God Is Within You

front: Work: Gandhi named this book one of the three most important in his life and founded a farm named after its author in South Africa
back: The Kingdom of God Is Within You (Leo Tolstoy)
work: The Kingdom of God Is Within You

front: Work: this work uses the folktale of a man hanging on a branch over a well with a dragon to analyze four approaches to suffering
Work: this aesthetic treatise attacks Wagner's Siegfried as 'counterfeit art' and praises Uncle Tom's Cabin and Millet's Man with the Hoe
Work: Dmitri Nekhlyudov pursues the marriage of Katusha in this novel; its author was excommunicated for it and donated profits to the Doukhobors
Author: this author was excommunicated from the Orthodox church by Konstantin Pobedonostsev
Author: this author died of pneumonia at a train station
Author: Maxim Gorky compared God and this owner of the estate Yasnaya Polyana to 'two bears in one den'
Author: Gorky called this author's crisis at an inn the 'Arzamas horror'
Author: this author is decried in the Russian Futurist manifesto 'A Slap in the Face of Public Taste'
Author: Marianne Moore's 'Poetry' quotes this author's diary expressing prejudice against 'business documents and school-books'
Author: this author became enamored with Baha'i after watching Isabella Grinevskaya's play about the Bab
Author: Rilke and Lou Andreas-Salome visited this author, who spent the meetings berating their writing
Author: Saul Bellow caused a stir by asking 'who is the Tolstoy of the Zulus?'
